chief_complaint: 53-year-old man brought in by ambulance after surviving an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
history: A 53-year-old man with no significant past cardiac history survived a cardiac arrest due to ventricular fibrillation (VF) at home. On arrival, he was haemodynamically stabilised. His 12-lead ECG at admission showed no Brugada-like ST elevation, early repolarisation pattern, or delta-wave in any lead. No prior episodes of syncope or palpitations were reported. No family history of sudden cardiac death. He was not on any regular medications.
exam: On examination: conscious and oriented after resuscitation. Heart rate 78 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, SpO2 98% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed regular heart sounds with no murmurs. Chest clear on auscultation. No peripheral oedema.
labs: ECG on admission: normal sinus rhythm, no ST-segment changes, no delta-wave, no early repolarisation. During therapeutic hypothermia: delta-wave became apparent across multiple leads, disappearing on re-warming. Echocardiogram: no structural heart disease, normal LV function (EF 60%). Electrophysiology study: no evidence of accessory pathway conduction. Troponin: mildly elevated post-arrest. Coronary angiogram: normal coronary arteries.
